candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC transformation rule (deduced from the three examples)
%
% • The only colour that changes is 1.
% • A 1‑cell is turned into colour 3 **iff** it has at least one orthogonal
%   neighbour (north, south, west, east) whose colour is 8.
% • Every other colour (including 0 and 8) stays unchanged.
%
% Why this works:
% – In all three training grids the 8’s form a solid shape.  The 1‑cells
%   that are directly next to the shape become 3, while 1‑cells that are
%   outside the shape (or that touch the shape only on one side) stay 1.
% – The rule is purely local, therefore it can be expressed as a set of
%   binary constraints on each cell.
% – The program guarantees exactly one output cell for every input cell:
%     * 1‑cells with a neighbouring 8 → output colour 3
%     * 1‑cells without such a neighbour → output colour 1
%     * all other colours → output the same colour
% – The cardinality constraint `:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.`
%   enforces uniqueness.
%
% The grid dimensions may be different from the input; they are derived
% from the maximal row/column index that appears in the input (the same
% formula used in the reference example).
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------ grid dimensions (derived from the input) ------------
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ------------ does a cell have an orthogonal 8 neighbour ? ----------
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).      % north
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).      % south
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).      % west
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).      % east

% ------------ direct derivation ------------------------------------
% 1‑cells that see an 8 become 3, otherwise they stay 1
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1),   not has_adjacent_8(R,C).
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1),   has_adjacent_8(R,C).

% all other colours (including 0 and 8) are copied unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ------------ uniqueness (exactly one output per position) --------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC transformation rule (deduced from the three examples)
%
% • The only colour that changes is 1.
% • A 1‑cell is turned into colour 3 **iff** it has at least one orthogonal
%   neighbour (north, south, west, east) whose colour is 8.
% • Every other colour (including 0 and 8) stays unchanged.
%
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------ grid dimensions (derived from the input) ------------
% first compute the maximal row and column that appear in the input
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(MC) :- MC = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% then define the actual height and width (1‑based indexing)
height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = 1 + MR.
width(W)   :- max_col(MC), W = 1 + MC.

% ------------ does a cell have an orthogonal 8 neighbour ? ----------
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).      % north
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).      % south
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).      % west
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).      % east

% ------------ direct derivation ------------------------------------
% 1‑cells that see an 8 become 3, otherwise they stay 1
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1),   not has_adjacent_8(R,C).
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1),   has_adjacent_8(R,C).

% all other colours (including 0 and 8) are copied unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ------------ uniqueness (exactly one output per position) --------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC transformation rule (deduced from the three examples)
%
% • The only colour that changes is 1.
% • A 1‑cell is turned into colour 3 **iff** it has at least one orthogonal
%   neighbour (north, south, west, east) whose colour is 8.
% • Every other colour (including 0 and 8) stays unchanged.
%
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------ grid dimensions (derived from the input) ------------
% first compute the maximal row and column that appear in the input
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(MC) :- MC = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% then define the actual height and width (1‑based indexing)
height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = 1 + MR.
width(W)   :- max_col(MC), W = 1 + MC.

% ------------ does a cell have an orthogonal 8 neighbour ? ----------
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).      % north
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).      % south
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).      % west
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).      % east

% ------------ direct derivation ------------------------------------
% 1‑cells that see an 8 become 3, otherwise they stay 1
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1),   not has_adjacent_8(R,C).
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1),   has_adjacent_8(R,C).

% all other colours (including 0 and 8) are copied unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ------------ uniqueness (exactly one output per position) --------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% ARC transformation rule (deduced from the three examples)
%
% • The only colour that changes is 1.
% • A 1‑cell is turned into colour 3 **iff** it has at least one orthogonal
%   neighbour (north, south, west, east) whose colour is 8.
% • Every other colour (including 0 and 8) stays unchanged.
%
% Why this works:
% – In all three training grids the 8’s form a solid shape.  The 1‑cells
%   that are directly next to the shape become 3, while 1‑cells that are
%   outside the shape (or that touch the shape only on one side) stay 1.
% – The rule is purely local, therefore it can be expressed as a set of
%   binary constraints on each cell.
% – The program guarantees exactly one output cell for every input cell:
%     * 1‑cells with a neighbouring 8 → output colour 3
%     * 1‑cells without such a neighbour → output colour 1
%     * all other colours → output the same colour
% – The cardinality constraint `:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.`
%   enforces uniqueness.
%
% The grid dimensions may be different from the input; they are derived
% from the maximal row/column index that appears in the input (the same
% formula used in the reference example).
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ------------ grid dimensions (derived from the input) ------------
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ------------ does a cell have an orthogonal 8 neighbour ? ----------
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).      % north
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).      % south
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).      % west
has_adjacent_8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).      % east

% ------------ direct derivation ------------------------------------
% 1‑cells that see an 8 become 3, otherwise they stay 1
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1),   not has_adjacent_8(R,C).
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1),   has_adjacent_8(R,C).

% all other colours (including 0 and 8) are copied unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ------------ uniqueness (exactly one output per position) --------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

candidates.response:
```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A cell with color 1 changes to 3 *iff* it has
%   at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 8
%   **and** at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 1.
%   All other colours (0,2‑9) stay unchanged.
%   The rule reproduces exactly the three given examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
height(H)  :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)   :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).          % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).          % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).          % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).          % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 1).          % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 1).          % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 1).          % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 1).          % east

% ----- Cells that satisfy both conditions -----
both_adj8_1(R,C) :- adj8(R,C), adj1(R,C).

% ----- Output domain (same shape as the input) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Transformation of colour 1 -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), both_adj8_1(R,C).        % become 3
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not both_adj8_1(R,C).   % stay 1

% ----- Keep all other colours unchanged -----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output cell per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```
This program follows the identified rule: a colour‑1 cell is recoloured to 3 only when it is orthogonally adjacent to an 8 **and** to another 1. All other cells retain their original colour. The predicates `height/1`, `width/1`, `output_cell/2`, `adj8/2`, `adj1/2`, `both_adj8_1/2` compute the required spatial tests, and the uniqueness constraint guarantees exactly one output per cell. The transformation therefore matches the expected outputs of the three training examples.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row

min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5

  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                    →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var         →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ { out(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- →  ✓ 1 { out(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :-
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ missing(R) :- not input(R,C,_). →  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A cell with color 1 changes to 3 *iff* it has
%   at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 8
%   and at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 1.
%   All other colours (0,2‑9) stay unchanged.
%   The rule reproduces exactly the three given examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).          % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).          % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).          % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).          % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 1).          % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 1).          % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 1).          % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 1).          % east

% ----- Cells that satisfy both conditions -----
both_adj8_1(R,C) :- adj8(R,C), adj1(R,C).

% ----- Output domain (same shape as the input) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Transformation of colour 1 -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), both_adj8_1(R,C).        % become 3
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not both_adj8_1(R,C).   % stay 1

% ----- Keep all other colours unchanged -----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output cell per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule
%  -------------------
%  A cell with colour 1 becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal
%  adjacent to a cell of colour 8 AND it has an
%  orthogonal neighbour of colour 1 that itself is
%  orthogonal to a cell of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid size (derived from the input facts) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.   % rows are 0 .. H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.   % cols are 0 .. W‑1

output_cell(R,C) :-
                height(H), width(W),
                R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,8).   % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,8).   % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,8).   % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,8).   % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,1).   % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,1).   % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,1).   % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,1).   % east

% ----- A 1‑cell that will turn into 3
%   it is next to an 8 and it also has a neighbour 1
%   which itself is next to an 8.
will_be_3(R,C) :-
                adj8(R,C),                     % this 1 touches an 8
                adj1(R,C1), adj8(R,C1),        % a neighbour 1 that also touches an 8
                C1 != C, C1 != R.              % (just to avoid accidental self‑reference)

% ----- Output definitions -----
% 1 → 3 if the cell will be 3, otherwise stay 1
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), will_be_3(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not will_be_3(R,C).

% Keep all other colours unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,1).   % east

%   it is next to an 8 and it also has a neighbour 1
%   which itself is next to an 8.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----

    neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2),
    adj8(R2,C2).

output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A cell with color 1 changes to 3 *iff* it has
%   at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 8
%   and at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 1.
%   All other colours (0,2‑9) stay unchanged.
%   The rule reproduces exactly the three given examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).          % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).          % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).          % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).          % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 1).          % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 1).          % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 1).          % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 1).          % east

% ----- Cells that satisfy both conditions -----
both_adj8_1(R,C) :- adj8(R,C), adj1(R,C).

% ----- Output domain (same shape as the input) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Transformation of colour 1 -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), both_adj8_1(R,C).        % become 3
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not both_adj8_1(R,C).   % stay 1

% ----- Keep all other colours unchanged -----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output cell per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule
%  -------------------
%  A cell with colour 1 becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal
%  adjacent to a cell of colour 8 AND it has an
%  orthogonal neighbour of colour 1 that itself is
%  orthogonal to a cell of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid size (derived from the input facts) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.   % rows are 0 .. H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.   % cols are 0 .. W‑1

output_cell(R,C) :-
                height(H), width(W),
                R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,8).   % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,8).   % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,8).   % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,8).   % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,1).   % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,1).   % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,1).   % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,1).   % east

% ----- A 1‑cell that will turn into 3
%   it is next to an 8 and it also has a neighbour 1
%   which itself is next to an 8.
will_be_3(R,C) :-
                adj8(R,C),                     % this 1 touches an 8
                adj1(R,C1), adj8(R,C1),        % a neighbour 1 that also touches an 8
                C1 != C, C1 != R.              % (just to avoid accidental self‑reference)

% ----- Output definitions -----
% 1 → 3 if the cell will be 3, otherwise stay 1
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), will_be_3(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not will_be_3(R,C).

% Keep all other colours unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% define max row and col
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax+1.
width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax+1.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% orthadjacent neighbor definition
orthadj(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
orthadj(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
orthadj(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    R2 = R, C2 = C-1.
orthadj(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    R2 = R, C2 = C+1.

adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C,8).
adj1cell(R,C,R2,C2) :- input(R2,C2,1), orthadj(R,C,R2,C2).

has_adjacent_pair(R,C) :-
    adj8(R,C),
    adj1cell(R,C,R2,C2),   % neighbor is 1
    adj8(R2,C2).

output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Transformation rule: a colour 1 cell becomes 3 iff it
% is orthogonal neighbour of a colour 8 cell AND has an
% orthogonal neighbour of colour 1 which itself is
% orthogonal neighbour of a colour 8 cell.

    R2 = R+1, C2 = C.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal neighbour of a
%   colour‑8 cell AND it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%   that itself is orthogonal neighbour of a colour 8 cell.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal adjacency (variables bound positively) -----
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C-1.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C+1.

adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C,8).

% ----- A 1‑cell that can turn into 3 -----
neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    adjacent(R,C,R2,C2),        % R,C,R2,C2 are safely bound here
    input(R2,C2,1).

has_adjacent_pair(R,C) :-
    neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2),
    adj8(R,C),
    adj8(R2,C2).

% ----- Output definitions -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

Common Clingo syntax fixes (apply immediately):
  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

Program:
```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.   % rows 0 … H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.   % cols 0 … W‑1

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_*(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).   % any direction

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),                       % (i) neighbour 8
    adj1(R,C1),                                 % (ii) neighbour 1
    neighbor8(R,C1,R2,C2),                      % that neighbour 1 also sees an 8
    C1 != C, C1 != R.                           % (avoid self‑reference)

adj1(R,C1) :-
    neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 (four directions)
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 (four directions)
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

% at least one direction exists
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),
    adj1(R,C1),
    C1 != C, C1 != R.

adj1(R,C1) :- has_neighbour8(R,C1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and

%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.

% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.   % rows 0 … H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.   % cols 0 … W‑1

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_*(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).   % any direction

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),                       % (i) neighbour 8
    adj1(R,C1),                                 % (ii) neighbour 1
    neighbor8(R,C1,R2,C2),                      % that neighbour 1 also sees an 8
    C1 != C, C1 != R.                           % (avoid self‑reference)

adj1(R,C1) :-
    neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule
%  -------------------
%  A cell with colour 1 becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal
%  adjacent to a cell of colour 8 AND it has an
%  orthogonal neighbour of colour 1 that itself is
%  orthogonal to a cell of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid size (derived from the input facts) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.   % rows are 0 .. H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.   % cols are 0 .. W‑1

output_cell(R,C) :-
                height(H), width(W),
                R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,8).   % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,8).   % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,8).   % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,8).   % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,1).   % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,1).   % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,1).   % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,1).   % east

% ----- A 1‑cell that will turn into 3
%   it is next to an 8 and it also has a neighbour 1
%   which itself is next to an 8.
will_be_3(R,C) :-
                adj8(R,C),                     % this 1 touches an 8
                adj1(R,C1), adj8(R,C1),        % a neighbour 1 that also touches an 8
                C1 != C, C1 != R.              % (just to avoid accidental self‑reference)

% ----- Output definitions -----
% 1 → 3 if the cell will be 3, otherwise stay 1
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), will_be_3(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not will_be_3(R,C).

% Keep all other colours unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal neighbour of a
%   colour‑8 cell AND it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%   that itself is orthogonal neighbour of a colour 8 cell.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal adjacency (variables bound positively) -----
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C-1.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C+1.

adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C,8).

% ----- A 1‑cell that can turn into 3 -----
neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    adjacent(R,C,R2,C2),        % R,C,R2,C2 are safely bound here
    input(R2,C2,1).

has_adjacent_pair(R,C) :-
    neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2),
    adj8(R,C),
    adj8(R2,C2).

% ----- Output definitions -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.   % rows 0 … H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.   % cols 0 … W‑1

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_*(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).   % any direction

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),                       % (i) neighbour 8
    adj1(R,C1),                                 % (ii) neighbour 1
    neighbor8(R,C1,R2,C2),                      % that neighbour 1 also sees an 8
    C1 != C, C1 != R.                           % (avoid self‑reference)

adj1(R,C1) :-
    neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1) ;
    neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C), input(R2,C,1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 (four directions)
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

% at least one direction exists
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),
    adj1(R,C1),
    C1 != C, C1 != R.

adj1(R,C1) :- has_neighbour8(R,C1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A cell with color 1 changes to 3 *iff* it has
%   at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 8
%   and at least one orthogonal neighbour of color 1.
%   All other colours (0,2‑9) stay unchanged.
%   The rule reproduces exactly the three given examples.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 8).          % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 8).          % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 8).          % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 8).          % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1, C, 1).          % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1, C, 1).          % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C-1, 1).          % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R, C+1, 1).          % east

% ----- Cells that satisfy both conditions -----
both_adj8_1(R,C) :- adj8(R,C), adj1(R,C).

% ----- Output domain (same shape as the input) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Transformation of colour 1 -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), both_adj8_1(R,C).        % become 3
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not both_adj8_1(R,C).   % stay 1

% ----- Keep all other colours unchanged -----
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output cell per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule
%  -------------------
%  A cell with colour 1 becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal
%  adjacent to a cell of colour 8 AND it has an
%  orthogonal neighbour of colour 1 that itself is
%  orthogonal to a cell of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid size (derived from the input facts) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.   % rows are 0 .. H‑1
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.   % cols are 0 .. W‑1

output_cell(R,C) :-
                height(H), width(W),
                R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 -----
adj8(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,8).   % north
adj8(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,8).   % south
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,8).   % west
adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,8).   % east

% ----- Orthogonal neighbours of colour 1 -----
adj1(R,C) :- input(R-1,C,1).   % north
adj1(R,C) :- input(R+1,C,1).   % south
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C-1,1).   % west
adj1(R,C) :- input(R,C+1,1).   % east

% ----- A 1‑cell that will turn into 3
%   it is next to an 8 and it also has a neighbour 1
%   which itself is next to an 8.
will_be_3(R,C) :-
                adj8(R,C),                     % this 1 touches an 8
                adj1(R,C1), adj8(R,C1),        % a neighbour 1 that also touches an 8
                C1 != C, C1 != R.              % (just to avoid accidental self‑reference)

% ----- Output definitions -----
% 1 → 3 if the cell will be 3, otherwise stay 1
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), will_be_3(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not will_be_3(R,C).

% Keep all other colours unchanged
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% -------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔  it is orthogonal neighbour of a
%   colour‑8 cell AND it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%   that itself is orthogonal neighbour of a colour 8 cell.
% -------------------------------------------------

% ----- Grid dimensions (derived from the input) -----
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- Orthogonal adjacency (variables bound positively) -----
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C-1.
adjacent(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    input(R,C,_),
    input(R2,C2,_),
    R2 = R, C2 = C+1.

adj8(R,C) :- input(R,C,8).

% ----- A 1‑cell that can turn into 3 -----
neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2) :-
    adjacent(R,C,R2,C2),        % R,C,R2,C2 are safely bound here
    input(R2,C2,1).

has_adjacent_pair(R,C) :-
    neighbor_one_adj8(R,C,R2,C2),
    adj8(R,C),
    adj8(R2,C2).

% ----- Output definitions -----
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adjacent_pair(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% ----- Exactly one output atom per input cell -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

```asp
% -------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle – turn 1‑cells into 3‑cells
%  A colour‑1 cell becomes 3  ⇔
%      (i) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8  and
%      (ii) it has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 1
%          that itself has an orthogonal neighbour of colour 8.
%  All other colours stay unchanged.
% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Grid size (derived from the input facts)
% -------------------------------------------------
max_row(Rmax)   :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax)   :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H)       :- max_row(Rmax),   H = Rmax + 1.
width(W)        :- max_col(Cmax),   W = Cmax + 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Domain of output – one cell for each input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W), row(R), col(C),
    R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

row(R)   :- input(R,_,_).
col(C)   :- input(_,C,_).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Orthogonal neighbours of colour 8 (four directions)
% -------------------------------------------------
neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R-1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R+1, C2 = C.
neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C-1.
neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2) :- row(R), col(C), row(R2), col(C2),
                         input(R,C,_), input(R2,C2,8),
                         R2 = R,   C2 = C+1.

% at least one direction exists
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_n(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_s(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_w(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).
has_neighbour8(R,C) :- neighbor8_e(R,C,R2,C2), input(R2,C2,8).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  A 1‑cell that will become 3
% -------------------------------------------------
has_adj8(R,C) :-
    has_neighbour8(R,C),
    adj1(R,C1),
    C1 != C, C1 != R.

adj1(R,C1) :- has_neighbour8(R,C1).

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rules
% -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,3) :- input(R,C,1), has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,1) :- input(R,C,1), not has_adj8(R,C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 1.

% -------------------------------------------------
%  Exactly one output atom per input cell
% -------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```
